intro_text: Hi there! 😊 Ready for Polynomials? These are algebraic expressions that follow special rules — and they unlock the secret to factorising any expression! Let's dive in! 🚀
complete_message: You have mastered Polynomials! The Remainder and Factor Theorems are now yours 💪

explain_questions.question: What is a polynomial? What are its key parts?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is a polynomial and what are its key parts?

steps.t: A polynomial is an algebraic expression with variables and coefficients, where exponents of variables are WHOLE NUMBERS (0, 1, 2, 3...). Example: 3x² + 5x − 7 is a polynomial.
steps.s: A polynomial uses whole number powers of variables. No fractions or negative powers allowed.

steps.t: Key parts: Term = each part separated by + or −. Coefficient = number multiplied with the variable. Degree = highest power of the variable. Constant = term with no variable.
steps.s: The degree is the highest power. The coefficient is the number before the variable.

steps.t: Types: Monomial (1 term), Binomial (2 terms), Trinomial (3 terms). And by degree: Linear (degree 1), Quadratic (degree 2), Cubic (degree 3).
steps.s: One term is monomial, two terms is binomial, three terms is trinomial.

explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: What is the degree of 5x cubed minus 4x squared plus 7x minus 2?
nudges: Look for the HIGHEST power of x
The highest power is 3, so degree = 3
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p1s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Polynomial: whole number powers only</text></g><g id="p1s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Monomial: 1 term | Binomial: 2 | Trinomial: 3</text></g><g id="p1s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Linear:deg1 | Quadratic:deg2 | Cubic:deg3</text></g><g id="p1ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">5x³ − 4x² + 7x − 2 → Degree = 3</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the zeroes of a polynomial?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the zeroes of a polynomial?

steps.t: A zero of a polynomial p(x) is a value of x that makes p(x) = 0. To find zeroes, set p(x) = 0 and solve for x.
steps.s: The zero of a polynomial is the value of x that makes the polynomial equal to zero.

steps.t: Example: p(x) = x − 5. Set x − 5 = 0 → x = 5. So 5 is the zero of this polynomial. Verify: p(5) = 5 − 5 = 0 ✓
steps.s: For x minus 5, setting it to zero gives x equals 5. That is the zero.

steps.t: A linear polynomial (degree 1) has exactly ONE zero. A quadratic has at most TWO zeroes. A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes.
steps.s: A linear polynomial has one zero. A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes.

explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Find the zero of p of x equals 3x minus 6.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p2s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Zero of p(x) = value where p(x) = 0</text></g><g id="p2s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">p(x)=x−5 → x=5 is zero | p(5)=0 ✓</text></g><g id="p2s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Degree n → at most n zeroes</text></g><g id="p2ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">3x−6=0 → x=2</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the Remainder Theorem?

steps.t: The Remainder Theorem says: When a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder equals p(a). No long division needed!
steps.s: The Remainder Theorem: when p of x is divided by x minus a, the remainder is p of a.

steps.t: Example: Find remainder when p(x) = x³ + 3x² − 5x + 4 is divided by (x − 1). By theorem: remainder = p(1) = 1 + 3 − 5 + 4 = 3.
steps.s: Just substitute a equals 1 into the polynomial. 1 plus 3 minus 5 plus 4 equals 3.

steps.t: Why does this work? Polynomial division gives: p(x) = (x − a) × q(x) + r. When x = a: p(a) = 0 × q(a) + r = r. Beautiful shortcut!
steps.s: When x equals a, the divisor becomes zero, leaving just the remainder.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Find the remainder when x³ + 1 is divided by (x + 1).
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Find the remainder when x cubed plus 1 is divided by x plus 1.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p3s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Remainder Theorem: divide p(x) by (x−a)</text></g><g id="p3s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Remainder = p(a) — no long division!</text></g><g id="p3s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">p(x)÷(x−1): remainder=p(1)=1+3−5+4=3</text></g><g id="p3ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">p(−1)=−1+1=0 → Remainder=0</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the Factor Theorem?

steps.t: The Factor Theorem is a special case of Remainder Theorem: (x − a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0.
steps.s: x minus a is a factor of p of x if and only if p of a equals zero.

steps.t: To check if (x − 2) is a factor: calculate p(2). If p(2) = 0, yes it is! If p(2) ≠ 0, no it is not.
steps.s: Substitute the value. If the result is zero, it is a factor.

steps.t: To find the value of unknown k: if (x − a) is given as a factor, use p(a) = 0 and solve. Example: If (x−1) is factor of 4x³−4x²+k, then p(1) = 0 → 4−4+k = 0 → k = 0.
steps.s: Set p of a equal to zero and solve for the unknown.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Is (x + 1) a factor of x³ + x² + x + 1? Yes or No.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Is x plus 1 a factor of x cubed plus x squared plus x plus 1?
nudges: Result = 0, so YES, (x+1) is a factor!
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p4s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Factor Theorem: (x−a) is factor iff p(a)=0</text></g><g id="p4s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">p(a)=0 → factor | p(a)≠0 → not a factor</text></g><g id="p4s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Find k: set p(a)=0 and solve</text></g><g id="p4ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">p(−1)=−1+1−1+1=0 → YES factor</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you use algebraic identities for polynomials?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you use algebraic identities for polynomials?

steps.t: The key identities to memorise: (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b². (a−b)² = a² − 2ab + b². (a+b)(a−b) = a² − b². These help expand AND factorise.
steps.s: The three key square identities. They work for both expansion and factorisation.

steps.t: Sum and difference of cubes: a³ + b³ = (a+b)(a²−ab+b²). a³ − b³ = (a−b)(a²+ab+b²). Critical for factorising cubic expressions!
steps.s: For cubes: a cubed plus b cubed equals a plus b times a squared minus ab plus b squared.

steps.t: Super identity: (a+b+c)² = a²+b²+c²+2ab+2bc+2ca. And if a+b+c=0, then a³+b³+c³ = 3abc!
steps.s: If a plus b plus c equals zero, then a cubed plus b cubed plus c cubed equals 3abc.

explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p5s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">(a+b)²=a²+2ab+b² | (a−b)²=a²−2ab+b²</text></g><g id="p5s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a³+b³=(a+b)(a²−ab+b²) | a³−b³=(a−b)(a²+ab+b²)</text></g><g id="p5s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a+b+c=0 → a³+b³+c³=3abc</text></g><g id="p5ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">(2x+3y)²=4x²+12xy+9y²</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you factorise quadratic polynomials?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you factorise quadratic polynomials?

steps.t: For ax² + bx + c, find two numbers p and q such that p + q = b (coefficient of x) and p × q = ac (product of a and c).
steps.s: Find two numbers that add to b and multiply to a times c.

steps.t: Example: x² − 5x + 6. Here a=1, b=−5, c=6. Need p+q = −5 and p×q = 6. So p=−2, q=−3.
steps.s: For x squared minus 5x plus 6, we need numbers that add to negative 5 and multiply to 6. Those are negative 2 and negative 3.

steps.t: Split: x² − 2x − 3x + 6 = x(x−2) − 3(x−2) = (x−2)(x−3). Always verify by multiplying back!
steps.s: Split the middle term, group, and factor out. Then verify by expanding back.

explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p6s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Factorise ax²+bx+c: find p,q where p+q=b, pq=ac</text></g><g id="p6s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">x²−5x+6: need sum=−5, product=6 → −2,−3</text></g><g id="p6s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">x²−2x−3x+6=x(x−2)−3(x−2)=(x−2)(x−3)</text></g><g id="p6ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">x²+7x+12=(x+3)(x+4)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you evaluate expressions using identities?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you evaluate expressions using identities?

steps.t: Smart trick: instead of calculating 99² directly, write 99 = (100 − 1) and use (a−b)² = a²−2ab+b².
steps.s: Write the number as a sum or difference of a round number, then apply the identity.

steps.t: 99² = (100−1)² = 10000 − 200 + 1 = 9801. Much faster than multiplying 99×99!
steps.s: 100 squared is 10000. Minus 200 plus 1 gives 9801.

steps.t: Similarly: 101² = (100+1)² = 10201. And 98 × 102 = (100−2)(100+2) = 10000−4 = 9996. Identities save time in exams!
steps.s: These identity shortcuts are very useful in exams. Always look for a round number nearby.

explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Use an algebraic identity to find 101 squared.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p7s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">99²→write as (100−1)²→use identity</text></g><g id="p7s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">=10000−200+1=9801</text></g><g id="p7s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">101²=10201 | 98×102=9996</text></g><g id="p7ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">(100+1)²=10000+200+1=10201</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the identity for (a+b+c)² and when is it useful?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the identity for a plus b plus c whole squared?

steps.t: (a+b+c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca. This is one of the most powerful identities in Class 9 algebra.
steps.s: The full expansion of a plus b plus c squared has 6 terms.

steps.t: It helps you find a²+b²+c² when you know a+b+c and ab+bc+ca. Formula: a²+b²+c² = (a+b+c)² − 2(ab+bc+ca).
steps.s: Subtract 2 times the sum of products from the square of the sum.

steps.t: Example: If a+b+c = 9 and ab+bc+ca = 26, then a²+b²+c² = 81 − 52 = 29. This type appears in CBSE board papers every year!
steps.s: 81 minus 52 equals 29. This exact type appears in board exams every year.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If a+b+c = 6 and ab+bc+ca = 11, find a²+b²+c²
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: If a plus b plus c equals 6 and ab plus bc plus ca equals 11, find a squared plus b squared plus c squared.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p8s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">(a+b+c)²=a²+b²+c²+2ab+2bc+2ca</text></g><g id="p8s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a²+b²+c²=(a+b+c)²−2(ab+bc+ca)</text></g><g id="p8s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a+b+c=9,ab+bc+ca=26 → 81−52=29</text></g><g id="p8ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">6²−2(11)=36−22=14</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you factorise sum/difference of cubes?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you factorise sum and difference of cubes?

steps.t: Sum of cubes: a³ + b³ = (a+b)(a² − ab + b²). Difference of cubes: a³ − b³ = (a−b)(a² + ab + b²). These must be memorised!
steps.s: Sum of cubes: a plus b times a squared minus ab plus b squared. Difference: a minus b times a squared plus ab plus b squared.

steps.t: Example: Factorise 8x³ + 27. Identify: 8x³ = (2x)³ and 27 = 3³. So a = 2x, b = 3.
steps.s: Recognise that 8x cubed is 2x whole cubed and 27 is 3 cubed.

steps.t: Apply formula: (2x+3)((2x)²−(2x)(3)+3²) = (2x+3)(4x²−6x+9). Always check by identifying perfect cubes first!
steps.s: Substituting gives 2x plus 3 times 4x squared minus 6x plus 9.

explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p9s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a³+b³=(a+b)(a²−ab+b²) | a³−b³=(a−b)(a²+ab+b²)</text></g><g id="p9s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">8x³+27=(2x)³+3³ → a=2x, b=3</text></g><g id="p9s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">=(2x+3)(4x²−6x+9)</text></g><g id="p9ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">x³−8=(x−2)(x²+2x+4)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: When a+b+c = 0, what is a³+b³+c³?
explain_questions.question_spoken: When a plus b plus c equals zero, what is a cubed plus b cubed plus c cubed?

steps.t: The identity: a³+b³+c³ − 3abc = (a+b+c)(a²+b²+c²−ab−bc−ca). This is a critical identity for Class 9.
steps.s: The identity for sum of cubes minus 3abc involves the factor a plus b plus c.

steps.t: If a+b+c = 0, then the right side = 0 × (anything) = 0. So a³+b³+c³ − 3abc = 0.
steps.s: When a plus b plus c equals zero, the whole right side becomes zero.

steps.t: Therefore: a³+b³+c³ = 3abc. If three numbers add to zero, their cubes always equal 3 times their product.
steps.s: If the three numbers sum to zero, their cubes equal 3 times their product.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If x+y+z = 0 and xyz = 5, find x³+y³+z³.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: If x plus y plus z equals zero and xyz equals 5, find x cubed plus y cubed plus z cubed.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="p10s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a³+b³+c³−3abc=(a+b+c)(a²+b²+c²−ab−bc−ca)</text></g><g id="p10s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">a+b+c=0 → RHS=0 → a³+b³+c³=3abc</text></g><g id="p10s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">xyz=5 → x³+y³+z³=3×5=15</text></g><g id="p10ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">x³+y³+z³=3xyz=3×5=15</text></g>

practice_questions.question: The degree of the polynomial 5x³ − 4x² + 7x − 2 is:
practice_questions.question_spoken: The degree of the polynomial 5x cubed minus 4x squared plus 7x minus 2 is?
practice_questions.trick: Degree = highest power. Scan all terms, find the biggest exponent. Here x³ is the highest.
practice_questions.alt: List all powers: x³ has power 3, x² has power 2, x has power 1, constant has power 0. Highest = 3.

practice_questions.question: Which of the following is a binomial of degree 3? A: x³+x²+1  B: x³+5  C: 3x²−2  D: x+x²
practice_questions.question_spoken: Which is a binomial of degree 3? A: x cubed plus x squared plus 1. B: x cubed plus 5. C: 3x squared minus 2. D: x plus x squared.
steps: Binomial = exactly 2 terms, Degree = highest power
A: 3 terms (trinomial) | C: degree 2 | D: degree 2
B: x³+5 has 2 terms and degree 3 ✓
practice_questions.trick: Bi = 2 terms. Check term count first. Then check degree. B has 2 terms and highest power 3.
practice_questions.alt: A has 3 terms (not binomial). C and D have degree 2 (not 3). Only B has both 2 terms AND degree 3.

practice_questions.question: Find the zero of p(x) = 3x − 6.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the zero of p of x equals 3x minus 6.
practice_questions.trick: Zero means p(x)=0. Solve the equation. 3x=6 gives x=2.
practice_questions.alt: Think: what value of x makes 3x−6 equal zero? When x=2: 3(2)−6=6−6=0 ✓

practice_questions.question: Find the remainder when p(x) = x³ + 3x² − 5x + 4 is divided by (x − 1).
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the remainder when x cubed plus 3x squared minus 5x plus 4 is divided by x minus 1.
practice_questions.trick: Remainder Theorem: substitute x=1 directly. No long division needed. 1+3−5+4=3.
practice_questions.alt: p(1)=1³+3(1)²−5(1)+4=1+3−5+4=3. The remainder when divided by (x−1) is always p(1).

practice_questions.question: Is (x − 2) a factor of p(x) = x³ − 3x + 2? Yes or No.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Is x minus 2 a factor of x cubed minus 3x plus 2?
practice_questions.trick: Factor Theorem: substitute x=2. If result is zero, it is a factor. Here 8−6+2=4≠0. So NO.

practice_questions.question: Using Factor Theorem, check if (x + 1) is a factor of x³ + x² + x + 1.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Is x plus 1 a factor of x cubed plus x squared plus x plus 1?
practice_questions.trick: (x+1) means substitute x=−1. If result is zero, it is a factor. Here −1+1−1+1=0 ✓

practice_questions.question: Expand: (2x + 3y)²
practice_questions.trick: Square first term, double product of both, square last term. (2x)²=4x², 2×2x×3y=12xy, (3y)²=9y².
practice_questions.alt: a=2x so a²=4x². b=3y so b²=9y². 2ab=2×2x×3y=12xy. Total: 4x²+12xy+9y².

practice_questions.question: Evaluate 99² using a suitable identity.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Evaluate 99 squared using a suitable algebraic identity.
practice_questions.alt: Think of 99 as close to 100. 100²=10000. Subtract 2×100=200. Add 1²=1. Result: 9801.

practice_questions.question: Factorise: x² − 5x + 6
practice_questions.trick: Product=6, sum=−5. Both negative. −2 and −3 work. Factors: (x−2)(x−3).

practice_questions.question: Factorise using identity: 8x³ + 27
practice_questions.question_spoken: Factorise 8x cubed plus 27 using algebraic identity.
practice_questions.trick: Spot the cubes! 8=2³ and 27=3³. So 8x³=(2x)³. Apply sum of cubes formula.
practice_questions.alt: a=2x, b=3. a²=4x², ab=6x, b²=9. Formula gives (2x+3)(4x²−6x+9).

practice_questions.question: If x = 2 and x = 0 are zeroes of p(x) = 2x³ − 5x² + ax + b, find a and b.
practice_questions.question_spoken: If x equals 2 and x equals 0 are zeroes of 2x cubed minus 5x squared plus ax plus b, find a and b.
practice_questions.trick: Substitute each zero into p(x)=0. x=0 gives b easily. Then x=2 gives a. Two equations, two unknowns.

practice_questions.question: If x+y+z = 0, prove that x³+y³+z³ = 3xyz.
practice_questions.question_spoken: If x plus y plus z equals zero, what is x cubed plus y cubed plus z cubed?
practice_questions.trick: The key identity: a³+b³+c³−3abc=(a+b+c)(rest). If sum=0, the whole thing=0. So cubes=3abc.
practice_questions.alt: The right side has (x+y+z) as a factor. Since that equals zero, the expression equals zero. Rearranging gives the result.

practice_questions.question: Find the value of k if (x − 1) is a factor of 4x³ + 3x² − 4x + k.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find k if x minus 1 is a factor of 4x cubed plus 3x squared minus 4x plus k.

practice_questions.question: If a+b+c = 9 and ab+bc+ca = 26, find a²+b²+c².
practice_questions.question_spoken: If a plus b plus c equals 9 and ab plus bc plus ca equals 26, find a squared plus b squared plus c squared.
practice_questions.trick: (a+b+c)²=sum of squares + 2×sum of products. So sum of squares=(a+b+c)²−2(ab+bc+ca)=81−52=29.

practice_questions.question: Find the remainder when p(x) = x⁴ − x³ + x² + 3 is divided by (x + 1).
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the remainder when x to the 4th minus x cubed plus x squared plus 3 is divided by x plus 1.
